one_line_takeaway: Embedding user histories as ICL prompt demonstrations in LLM recommenders creates a real, black-box membership-inference leak: two prompt-structure-aware attacks (item-memorization and recommendation-inertia) hit near-perfect AUC on MovieLens-1M and survive existing prompt-extraction defenses.
key_finding: Both novel attacks are highly effective and consistently beat baselines. On MovieLens-1M log-scale ROC, ItemMem reaches AUC 1.000 (GPT-OSS:120b), 0.985 (Mistral), 0.703 (Llama4); RecInertia reaches 0.999 (Mistral), 0.978 (GPT-OSS:120b), 0.759 (Llama4). The Similarity baseline fails (member/non-member similarity distributions are indistinguishable); the Inquiry baseline works without defense but is almost fully neutralized by input prompt-sanitization. Existing prompt-extraction defenses do not adequately stop ItemMem/RecInertia: input sanitization fails in most cases and output filtering helps only sometimes (e.g., Beauty). Newer models (GPT-OSS, Llama4) appear MORE vulnerable than older ones. Factor analysis: ItemMem stays effective across 1/5/10 shots while RecInertia weakens with more shots (attention dilution); RecInertia effectiveness DECREASES as more items are poisoned (excessive poisoning makes the model rely on new context rather than old memory); performance is stable across positions until the last shot. Pretraining memorization is a weak confound: recall-the-k-th-interaction rates on ML-1M are only 0.03% (Llama:8b), 0.18% (Mistral), 0.22% (GPT-OSS:120b), and unmeasurable on Book/Beauty, so membership signal is dominated by prompt content, not pretraining.
core_contribution: The authors present the first study of membership inference attacks (MIAs) against in-context-learning (ICL) based LLM recommender systems, where private user-item interaction histories are embedded as demonstrations directly in the system prompt at inference time (rather than in trained model parameters as in traditional RecSys). Their OWN contribution is TWO novel black-box attacks that exploit structural features unique to ICL RecSys: (1) ItemMem (Item Memorization), which issues a normal recommendation query and counts how many items the model repeats from the victim's historical recommended-item set R_h^u, classifying the victim as a member when repeats exceed a threshold tau_m; and (2) RecInertia (Recommendation Inertia), which submits queries with the victim's interaction history perturbed by low-similarity item substitutions and measures whether the model's recommendations stay 'inert' (resistant to perturbation) via cosine similarity of Sentence-Transformer embeddings against a threshold tau_p. They also show existing prompt-extraction defenses (prompt sanitization, output filtering) do not reliably stop these attacks. NOTE: the two traditional-RecSys MIA families (Item-Diff / DL-MIA / similarity- and shadow-model-based methods by Zhang, Wang, Wei, Zhong et al.) are cited Related Work, NOT this paper's contribution; the paper's 'Similarity Attack' is a re-implemented baseline that performs poorly on ICL RecSys.
threat_model: Black-box adversary whose goal is to decide whether a specific victim user u appears among the k demonstration users {u_1,...,u_k} embedded in an ICL RecSys system prompt. Following the black-box RecSys MIA setting of Zhang et al. [48], the adversary knows the victim's historical interactions and sample recommendations, and can query the target LLM and observe its recommended items, but has NO access to the tokenizer or output token probabilities (posterior-free). The adversary may use general-purpose word/semantic embeddings from OTHER open-source LLMs (not the target model), e.g., Sentence-Transformer, to compute similarities. The unknown to the adversary is whether the provider actually used the victim's data to compose the prompt.
limitations: Evaluation uses a small balanced set of 100 cases per configuration and reports 'best-performing' thresholds tuned on a validation set (tau_m ~6-10 for ItemMem, tau_p 0.6-0.85 for RecInertia), which may overstate real-world worst-case-adversary advantage. Only open-source LLMs and three classic academic datasets are tested; no production/commercial RecSys. Demonstrations are synthetic (LightGCN-generated) rather than real deployed prompts. The paper proposes no new tailored defense (only evaluates existing prompt-extraction defenses) and only gestures at DP prompts as future work while acknowledging utility loss. Detailed per-dataset/per-model numbers are largely relegated to figures and an anonymized code repo; the PDF (an unfinished submission with placeholder ACM template fields and 2018 boilerplate) exposes few exact scalar values in text. RecInertia depends on external semantic embeddings whose quality bounds the attack.

quantitative_results: ItemMem log-scale ROC on Movie: AUC = 1.000 (GPT-OSS:120b), 0.985 (Mistral), 0.703 (Llama4)
RecInertia log-scale ROC on Movie: AUC = 0.999 (Mistral), 0.978 (GPT-OSS:120b), 0.759 (Llama4)
Optimal thresholds: ItemMem tau_m ~6-10 repeated items; RecInertia tau_p in 0.6-0.85
Pretraining-data memorization on ML-1M (recall k-th interaction): Llama:8b 0.03%, Mistral 0.18%, GPT-OSS:120b 0.22%; no measurable memorization on Book/Beauty
Attack advantage defined as Adv = 2*(Acc - 0.5); 0 = random, 1 = perfect
Evaluation: 100 balanced (member, non-member, demonstrations) test cases per setting; shots tested at 1/5/10; poisoned items tested at 1/5/10
RecInertia advantage consistently decreases as the number of poisoned items increases; ItemMem stays stable across 1/5/10 shots
baselines_compared: Direct Inquiry (prompt-stealing 'Have you seen a user interacted with I_u? Yes/No') - strong without defense but nearly eliminated by input prompt-sanitization

Similarity Attack (re-implemented traditional-RecSys similarity MIA using Sentence-Transformer semantic embeddings + cosine similarity) - consistently poor; member/non-member distributions indistinguishable
Existing defenses evaluated (not baselines but comparison conditions): prompt sanitization (input) and gatekeeper-style output-filtering agent
